body: A relative pulls up a chart on their phone: skin-cancer diagnoses have risen steadily since the 1980s — right as sunscreen went mainstream.
“Explain that. We all started slathering on sunscreen, and skin cancer went up. It's obviously not protecting anyone — for all we know it's causing it.”
question: What's the strongest response to this chart?

statements.t: The timing is hard to ignore — if sunscreen worked, rates should have fallen. It's fair to suspect the stuff plays some role in all this.
Both lines can be pushed up by something else entirely: sun holidays, tanning beds, far better screening. Two trends together never settle causation.
You cannot learn anything at all from a chart of two trends. There is nothing here worth explaining, and the whole argument can be set aside.

explain.tempting: A tight match in timing feels like a smoking gun — our minds are built to read sequence as cause. And “the official advice is backwards” is a satisfying story.
explain.clean: Both lines can be pushed up by third factors: decades of cheap sun holidays and tanning beds (skin cancer takes 20+ years to show up), plus far better screening finding cases that were previously missed. And the highest-risk people use the most sunscreen, which alone links sunscreen to cancer in the data. When researchers compare similar people with and without sunscreen in controlled trials, sunscreen reduces melanoma.
explain.rule: When two things rise together, ask: what else rose too? And who ended up in each group?

sources.label: Green et al. 2011 — randomized trial: sunscreen reduced melanoma (J Clin Oncol)
US melanoma incidence trends (NCI SEER)

body: Your back has hurt for three weeks. At its absolute worst — you can barely tie your shoes — you finally try the expensive herbal patch a friend swears by.
Four days later you're dramatically better. “See?” says the friend. “Everyone I send to that patch says the same thing.”
question: What can you actually conclude?

statements.t: The pain was at its worst, you used the patch, and within days the pain went. Your own body is better evidence than all the studies in the world.
You reached for it at rock bottom, and flare-ups usually improve from their peak unaided, so almost anything tried then looks like a cure.
Nothing about four days of recovery can be credited to a patch. This was pure coincidence, and there is nothing further worth investigating here.

explain.tempting: Nothing feels more like proof than your own body. Pain, patch, relief — the sequence writes the story for you, and everyone your friend “sends to the patch” lives the same sequence.
explain.clean: You don't reach for a new remedy on an average day — you reach for it at the peak. And peaks, by definition, are usually followed by improvement (statisticians call it regression to the mean). That's why anything tried at the worst moment “works”: the patch, the copper bracelet, the lucky socks. Only a comparison — similar sufferers with and without the patch — can separate the remedy from the rebound. Note the “no active ingredient, so pure coincidence” answer makes the mirror-image mistake: certainty without the comparison.
explain.rule: You try cures when things are at their worst — and “worst” usually improves by itself.

sources.label: Barnett et al. 2005 — regression to the mean in health research (Int J Epidemiol)

body: The chat turns to a news story about smoking. Someone writes:
“My grandfather smoked a pack a day and lived to 94, sharp as a razor. My aunt never touched a cigarette and got lung cancer at 60. Makes you wonder if the whole smoking thing is overblown.”
question: What do these two family stories tell you?

statements.t: Two counterexamples this close to home are fair reason to suspect the risk is exaggerated — everyone knows a case like grandpa's.
Smoking raises risk without guaranteeing outcomes, so among millions of smokers a sharp 94-year-old is expected. The ones it caught aren't at dinner talking.
Family stories are the weakest form of evidence there is. Personal experience of this kind belongs outside a serious discussion about health risks.

explain.tempting: Stories from your own family feel like data you personally verified — and one vivid exception seems to poke a hole in the rule.
explain.clean: “Raises your risk” is a claim about rates, not a promise about each person. With millions of smokers, long-lived smokers must exist — and they're the ones still around to be quoted. The ones the risk caught died younger, and the dead don't tell their side at dinner. That's survivorship bias: the exceptions are systematically the ones you get to hear from. The rates — comparing many smokers to many non-smokers — already include grandpa and everyone less lucky.
explain.rule: The counterexamples you hear from are the survivors. Ask what happened to everyone else.

sources.label: Doll et al. 2004 — 50-year cohort of British doctors (BMJ)

body: A headline: “Daily processed meat raises bowel-cancer risk by 18%.”
The figure comes from the WHO's cancer agency, which reviewed hundreds of studies to reach it. In your group chat, one friend is swearing off bacon forever.
question: What does that 18% actually mean?

statements.t: Eating bacon daily gives you roughly an 18% chance of bowel cancer — nearly one in five, which is an enormous risk to accept.
That's a relative increase: lifetime risk moves from about 6 in 100 to about 7 in 100. Small, real, and now yours to weigh.
Headlines like this are built to alarm rather than inform. The sensible response is to disregard the figure entirely and carry on eating exactly as before.

explain.tempting: “Raises risk by 18%” slides straight into “my risk is 18%.” Headlines love relative numbers precisely because they sound enormous while staying technically true.
explain.clean: An 18% increase is 18% of the starting risk. Lifetime bowel-cancer risk is around 6% — so daily processed meat moves it to about 7%. One extra case per hundred daily eaters: real, worth knowing, and completely different from “nearly one in five.” The same trick runs in both directions — “cuts risk in half!” can mean going from 2 in 10,000 to 1 in 10,000. Neither panic nor blanket cynicism is needed; the absolute numbers let you decide like an adult.
explain.rule: “X% more” — of what? Never react to a relative number until you've seen the absolute one.

sources.label: WHO/IARC Q&A — processed meat and cancer risk
Lifetime bowel cancer risk (Cancer Research UK)

body: A slick documentary features twelve scientists and MDs arguing that a widely used class of medicines is a scam kept alive by industry money.
A friend is shaken: “These are real doctors with real credentials. Twelve of them! You can't just wave that away.”
question: How much should twelve on-camera experts move you?

statements.t: Every one of them holds a real qualification, and a dozen willing to say this on camera means the mainstream position is in trouble.
Millions hold those credentials, so a dozen dissenters can be cast for almost any claim. The film shows its casting, not the field.
Documentaries are built to persuade rather than to inform. This genre is best skipped entirely, and the people who appear in it treated as paid performers.

explain.tempting: Credentials are our honest shortcut for “this person knows things” — and twelve of them feels like a consensus. A film never shows you the selection process that found them.
explain.clean: The question is never “can credentialed dissenters be found?” — they can, for any position, because millions of people hold credentials. The question is what the whole field concludes and what the dissenters have besides interviews: published data survives scrutiny, camera time doesn't. A useful check: does the film tell you how many experts hold the opposite view? A curated dozen is a casting decision wearing a lab coat.
explain.rule: You can find a PhD for any claim. Ask what the field concludes, not who the camera found.

sources.label: FLICC — fake experts and the five techniques of science denial

body: A viral post: “A local hospital admits that 60% of patients hospitalized with virus X this winter were vaccinated against it. Still think it works?”
The number, it turns out, is accurate.
question: Does this show the vaccine isn't working?

statements.t: If 60% of the people filling hospital beds got the shot, then whatever protection it offers is clearly weak — the number speaks plainly.
Where 90% of the elderly have been vaccinated, vaccinated patients can fill a ward even while their individual risk stays far lower.
Once you allow for how many people are vaccinated, this figure actually demonstrates the vaccine is working, and working extremely well.

explain.tempting: “Most of the sick were vaccinated” sounds like the vaccine failed an obvious test. The number is true, vivid, and feels complete — what more could you need?
explain.clean: The missing piece is: vaccinated out of how many? Imagine 90 of 100 elderly people are vaccinated. Even if vaccination cuts hospitalization risk to a quarter, the ward will still hold more vaccinated than unvaccinated people — because there are nine times as many of them to draw from. The informative comparison is hospitalizations per 10,000 vaccinated vs. per 10,000 unvaccinated. A true percentage with a hidden denominator is the internet's favorite weapon. The mirror error overreaches just as badly: once you account for the denominator, this figure stops arguing against the vaccine — it does not start proving the vaccine works.
explain.rule: A share of the hospitalized means nothing without the share of everyone. Ask: out of how many?

sources.label: Morris — why most hospitalized patients can be vaccinated while vaccines work (base rates worked example)

body: A study finds that teenagers who use social media more than four hours a day are twice as likely to be depressed.
The headline: “Proof at last: phones are destroying our kids.” The study itself measured both things at one point in time.
question: What does the study actually show?

statements.t: Twice the depression rate is far too large a gap to explain away. Everyone can see the phones are doing something real here.
The two go together in this data, but the arrow could run either way, and loneliness could drive both. A snapshot cannot settle direction.
Cross-sectional research is far too weak to build anything on. Until better designs arrive, the responsible reading is that screens and teenage depression are unrelated.

explain.tempting: “Twice as likely” plus a story we already believe — of course it reads as proof. The size of a correlation feels like evidence of causation, but it isn't; it's evidence of a strong link.
explain.clean: A snapshot can't tell you which came first. Maybe scrolling erodes mood; maybe low mood drives scrolling (comfort, insomnia, withdrawal from friends); maybe loneliness drives both. The honest reading: a genuine link, direction unknown, worth the better studies that can untangle it — and “worthless, so no connection” overshoots just as far in the other direction.
explain.rule: Before believing A causes B, ask: could B cause A? Could C cause both?

sources.label: Orben & Przybylski 2019 — screens and well-being, association sizes (Nature Human Behaviour)

body: A colleague, reaching for the tissues: “Never again. I got the flu shot in October, and two days later I was sick as a dog for a week.
That's not a coincidence — the shot gave it to me.”
question: What's the strongest assessment?

statements.t: Two days is far too tight a window for coincidence, and the simplest explanation is usually the right one: the shot made him ill for a week.
Millions get the shot exactly as virus season begins, so huge numbers fall ill soon afterwards purely by chance, whatever the injection does.
An inactivated vaccine cannot make anyone ill, so his week in bed had nothing whatever to do with the injection he received.

explain.tempting: Tight timing feels like a fingerprint. And your colleague isn't lying — he really did get sick two days after the shot. The experience is true; the inference is the mistake.
explain.clean: Flu shots are given by the millions at the exact moment cold-and-flu season ramps up — so “sick within a week of the shot” happens to huge numbers of people every year no matter what the shot does. (A day of feeling off from the immune response is real; a week of full flu from an inactivated vaccine isn't how the biology works.) The only way to see the shot's true effect is to compare groups. One vivid personal sequence, honestly reported, is exactly what coincidence looks like at scale.
explain.rule: “After this, therefore because of this” — ask how many people got the same “after” by chance alone.

sources.label: CDC — misconceptions about flu vaccines (inactivated vaccine cannot cause flu)

body: An influencer sells a “focus” supplement. The pinned post has thousands of comments: “changed my life,” “brain fog gone in a week,” “ordered three more bottles.”
A friend forwards it: “There's no way thousands of people are all lying.”
question: Are thousands of glowing testimonials strong evidence it works?

statements.t: A few reviews can be planted, but thousands of detailed, independent testimonials can't all be wrong about the same product at once.
Buyers who felt nothing rarely comment, critical ones are often deleted, and expectation does real work. A sugar pill collects thousands of sincere raves.
A comment section like this is worth little as evidence — the sensible default is to assume the product does not work until a proper trial says otherwise.

explain.tempting: “They can't all be lying” is true — and that's exactly what makes it persuasive. The testimonials are mostly sincere. What's broken isn't the people; it's the filter that decides which people you hear.
explain.clean: Suppose 100,000 people buy it. Some were about to have a good week anyway; some feel the boost that comes from expecting one. Even if the pills are inert, thousands now sincerely believe it worked — and they're the ones who comment, get pinned, and get shown to you. The disappointed majority quietly doesn't reorder. A comment section is not a sample; it's a highlight reel curated by enthusiasm and the seller's delete button. Note the “bought and curated” answer reaches the right verdict by a lazy route — it collapses the moment the testimonials turn out to be sincere.
explain.rule: Testimonials are a filtered sample: you see the hits, never the misses.

sources.label: Harvard Health — the power of the placebo effect

body: A post shows a chart of global temperatures covering the last eight years. The line is roughly flat. Caption: “The warming stopped. They just don't update you.”
You check: the data points in the chart are real.
question: The data is real — so what's the right takeaway?

statements.t: Real data is real data. If eight years show no rise, the trend has at least paused, whatever the longer charts say.
A short window beginning at a record spike can look flat inside a rising trend, so ask who chose that particular start year.
Charts circulated on social media are selected for effect, so the sensible policy is to rely only on official statistical agencies.

explain.tempting: “The data is real” feels like the end of the argument. But whoever picks the window picks the story the real data tells — start at a record-hot spike and the next few years look flat by design.
explain.clean: Noisy upward trends are full of stretches that look flat or even falling — pick your start and end points carefully and you can “show” anything. The test isn't whether the points are real; it's whether the window was chosen for you. Zoom out to the full record and the stretch dissolves into the trend. The identical trick sells investment funds (“up 40% since [carefully chosen month]”) and both attacks and defends every policy under the sun. And note: the fix is a method — zoom out — not the shortcut of trusting or distrusting whole categories of sources.
explain.rule: Whoever picks the window picks the conclusion. Zoom out.

sources.label: Skeptical Science — 'the escalator': cherry-picked windows in a noisy trend
NASA — global temperature record

body: Three break-in posts this month, each with hundreds of alarmed comments. More neighbors are posting doorbell footage of “suspicious” passers-by every day.
A neighbor tells you: “This area has gone downhill fast. Crime is exploding — everyone can see it.”
question: What can you conclude about local crime?

statements.t: When this many neighbors are seeing it firsthand, the rise is real — everyone here can feel the shift before a statistic catches up.
One incident spawns many posts, alarm drives more cameras and more posting, and incidents themselves can stay flat. Posts measure attention, not crime.
Recorded crime figures depend entirely on what people bother to report — nobody can say whether this neighborhood is safer or more dangerous than before.

explain.tempting: This one runs on good instincts: trusting your own eyes and your neighbors. But you're not seeing the neighborhood — you're seeing what an engagement-ranked feed selected from it.
explain.clean: Alarm is a feedback loop: one break-in produces a post, the post produces fear, fear produces more posts and more cameras and more “suspicious person” reports — the signal multiplies while the thing itself needn't change at all. Three real incidents can feel like a crime wave. The feed measures how alarmed people are, not how dangerous the area is; those can move independently. Local police statistics over several years are checkable in minutes — and “impossible to know” is its own mistake when the answer is a search away.
explain.rule: A feed is a fear amplifier, not a measuring instrument. Alarming posts can multiply while the thing itself doesn't.

sources.label: Pew Research — public perception vs. measured crime rates

body: After a new nuclear plant is proposed nearby, a widely shared post lists Chernobyl and Fukushima, with photos, and concludes:
“This is the deadliest form of energy ever created. No sane country builds more of it.”
question: What's the clean way to judge that claim?

statements.t: Chernobyl and Fukushima showed what this technology does when it fails. That record matters more than any per-unit statistic ever could.
Deaths per unit of energy count both the famous accidents and the quiet toll of fossil-fuel air pollution, and on that measure nuclear ranks among the safest.
Nuclear power's safety record is settled, so continuing to worry about the plant is a straightforwardly irrational response to the evidence.

explain.tempting: Named disasters with photos are unforgettable — and memory feels like evidence. A danger you can picture vividly seems bigger than one that kills quietly, one case of lung disease at a time, with no famous incident to remember.
explain.clean: Vivid events measure how memorable a danger is, not how large. The fair comparison is deaths per unit of energy produced: rare spectacular accidents on one side, decades of routine air-pollution deaths from coal and gas on the other. On that measure, nuclear sits near the bottom alongside wind and solar. You can still oppose the plant — cost, waste, whatever weighs with you — but “I can name its disasters” is a fact about your memory, not about the risk. (And “completely safe, you're irrational” overshoots in the other direction: real trade-offs exist.)
explain.rule: Memorable is not the same as common. Divide by the denominator before comparing dangers.

sources.label: Our World in Data — deaths per unit of energy, by source

body: Another parent shares an article: kids who take music lessons score significantly higher in school.
“That settles it — we're signing up this week. It literally makes them smarter.”
question: What does the link between music lessons and grades show?

statements.t: Music training builds discipline and focus, which is exactly why lesson-takers do better. Signing up should deliver a genuine academic boost.
Families who choose and afford lessons differ already — income, time, tutoring, expectations — and those differences lift grades all by themselves.
Education research cannot randomize children into families. Findings in this field sit closer to horoscopes than to evidence a parent should act on.

explain.tempting: It's a link that flatters something we'd love to believe: that there's a purchasable lever for our kids' success. And a plausible mechanism — discipline, practice, focus — makes the causal leap feel safe.
explain.clean: Kids aren't randomly assigned to music lessons. The families who choose them already differ before the first note — income, stable schedules, parental involvement, tutoring — and those same differences raise grades on their own. When researchers account for family background, or randomly assign actual music training, the “music makes you smarter” effect shrinks toward zero. Music is still great — for music reasons. The overshoot errs the other way: careful studies of kids can and do tell us things; this particular headline just isn't one of them.
explain.rule: Ask who ends up in each group — and whether that difference alone could explain the gap.

sources.label: Sala & Gobet 2020 — meta-analysis: music training's cognitive benefits are null once design quality is controlled (Memory & Cognition)

body: A friend, glowing: “I cut gluten six weeks ago and I'm a different person — more energy, no more bloating, better skin.
You should really try it. Bread was basically poisoning me.”
question: How solid is “I cut gluten and felt better”?

statements.t: Six weeks of feeling dramatically better after one deliberate change is about as direct as evidence gets — and she is the one living in her body.
Cutting gluten changes a dozen things at once — less junk food, more cooking, more attention — so only a blinded test isolates gluten.
Symptom reports collected without a control group are unreliable, so her improvement is best understood as expectation and attention rather than anything her diet actually did.

explain.tempting: The improvement is real, and it followed the change — the sequence tells the story for you. And she did something hard, so “it worked” also rewards the effort.
explain.clean: “Going gluten-free” is one label for a dozen simultaneous changes: dropping pastries, beer, and fast food, cooking more, and paying attention to your body. Add the placebo effect — genuinely strong for energy and gut symptoms — and starting at a low point (people overhaul their diet when they feel worst), and feeling better afterward is expected no matter what gluten does. In blinded studies, most people who report gluten sensitivity can't tell gluten capsules from placebo — and some can, which is why the careful test matters. Filing it all under expectation gets it wrong too: the improvement is real; only the credit is in question.
explain.rule: One change on the label, a dozen changes in practice. Ask what else changed at the same time.

sources.label: Molina-Infante & Carroccio 2017 — blinded gluten challenges confirm few suspected cases; strong nocebo response (Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol)

body: A healthy friend took a screening test for a rare condition — about 1 in 1,000 people have it. The test catches 99% of true cases, and wrongly flags about 2% of healthy people. Hers came back positive.
“The test is 99% accurate,” she says. “So it's basically certain I have it.”
question: Given the positive result, how worried should she be?

statements.t: The test is 99% accurate, so a positive result means it is roughly 99% likely that she actually has the condition.
Among 1,000 people like her, about one truly has it and twenty healthy people get flagged. Her positive is one of twenty-one.
A test that wrongly flags 2% of healthy people produces far more false alarms than real cases, so her positive carries no useful information.

explain.tempting: “99% accurate” sounds like it describes her situation. But accuracy describes the test's behavior across many people — on its own it says nothing about what one positive means when the condition is rare.
explain.clean: Rarity dominates. In 1,000 people, the test finds the 1 real case — and also flags about 20 of the 999 healthy people by mistake. Twenty-one positives, one real: under 5%. This is exactly why medicine follows screening positives with confirmatory tests instead of treating on the spot. And “ignore it” is wrong in the other direction: her risk did jump from 0.1% to about 5% — fifty times higher — which is precisely worth the follow-up and nothing more yet.
explain.rule: When the condition is rare, most positives are false. Run the 1,000-people picture before panicking.

sources.label: Gigerenzer et al. — helping doctors and patients make sense of health statistics (Psychol Sci Public Interest)

body: Two options for dinner: a new place with a perfect 5.0 from its first 12 reviews, and the old neighborhood favorite holding 4.3 across 2,800 reviews.
Your friend: “5.0 beats 4.3 — the new place is clearly better.”
question: Which rating is stronger evidence of a good meal?

statements.t: A perfect score is a perfect score. Twelve people agreeing completely beats an average diluted by thousands of mixed opinions over the years.
Twelve reviews is an early, friendly sample, and small samples swing to extremes. Across 2,800 reviews, luck washes out and the average reflects a typical evening.
Neither number is informative here. The sensible way to choose is to ignore the ratings and pick whichever place you like the look of.

explain.tempting: 5.0 > 4.3 is the easiest math in the world. What the comparison hides is that the two numbers carry completely different amounts of evidence.
explain.clean: Small samples are extreme samples. The first dozen reviewers of a new spot are disproportionately friends, well-wishers, and people who caught the chef's best night — and even by pure chance, a dozen ratings can average almost anywhere. Across 2,800 reviews, luck and superfans wash out and the number converges on the real typical experience. This is the same reason the smallest counties post both the highest and lowest disease rates, and why “miracle” results tend to come from the smallest studies. (The new place might be great — the honest answer is that twelve reviews can't tell you yet.)
explain.rule: Before comparing two averages, compare their sample sizes. Extremes live in small samples.

sources.label: Wainer — the most dangerous equation: small samples produce extremes (American Scientist)

body: Headline: “Dog attacks in the city DOUBLED last year.”
Further down, the figures: from 4 reported attacks the year before to 8, in a city of 600,000 people.
question: What does “doubled” amount to here?

statements.t: Doubling is doubling. A 100% rise in a single year means something has clearly changed, and everyone in the city should know about it.
Four becoming eight sits well inside ordinary year-to-year noise, moving the per-person risk from about 1-in-150,000 to about 1-in-75,000.
Four cases becoming eight in a city of 600,000 tells you nothing whatever, so this article is filler dressed up as news.

explain.tempting: Percentages feel like magnitudes: “100% increase” sounds like a city transformed. The smaller the starting number, the bigger and scarier the percentage swings look — which is exactly why headlines love small bases.
explain.clean: Counts of rare events bounce hard from year to year by chance alone — 4 to 8 is the kind of swing you'd expect even if nothing about dogs, owners, or the city changed. (From 4,000 to 8,000 would be a genuine signal; from 4 to 8 is closer to a coin's whim.) Two questions defuse the headline: what are the absolute numbers, and what does the multi-year trend show? The overshoot cuts the other way — small numbers aren't no information, they're weak information: a jump to 80 would matter immediately.
explain.rule: The smaller the base, the louder the percentage. Ask for absolute numbers and the multi-year trend.

sources.label: Tversky & Kahneman 1971 — belief in the law of small numbers

body: The council proposes apartment buildings near the station. Your feed is wall-to-wall opposition — angry posts, a petition, furious comment threads.
A neighbor: “Literally everyone is against this. The council is ignoring the entire town.”
question: Is the whole town against it?

statements.t: Every post, comment, and petition you have seen points one way. That is the town speaking, and the opposition is overwhelming.
Feeds surface the angriest and most motivated, while the content majority says nothing. You are seeing who is loudest, not how many.
Online sentiment tends to invert what people privately think. The quiet majority probably supports this plan far more than the petition would suggest.

explain.tempting: Unanimity in your feed feels like unanimity in the world — dozens of real people visibly agree, and nobody visible disagrees.
explain.clean: Three filters sit between the town and your screen: people with strong feelings post far more than the content majority; platforms amplify whatever gets angry engagement; and your network resembles you. Together they can turn a 25% opinion into 95% of what you see — sincerely, with nobody lying. The visible side isn't the popular side; it's the motivated side. Representative surveys exist precisely because feeds can't do this job. And no — a skewed feed doesn't mean the truth is the opposite; it means the feed simply can't tell you.
explain.rule: Your feed shows who's loudest, not how many. “Unanimous online” is a fact about the filter, not the town.

sources.label: Pew Research — a small share of users produce the large majority of posts

body: A widely shared post: “People who regularly take prescription sleeping pills die years earlier than people who don't. They don't want you to know.”
The underlying study is real: regular users did have notably higher mortality over the follow-up period.
question: Does the study show sleeping pills shorten lives?

statements.t: Higher death rates among users is exactly what a harmful drug produces. The study measures the risk about as directly as possible.
People prescribed sleeping pills already differ in chronic illness, pain, depression and severe insomnia, so the study cannot separate the pills from the reasons for them.
Observational mortality data cannot be cleaned up after the fact, so medication safety is knowable only inside a properly randomized trial.

explain.tempting: The comparison feels clean — users versus non-users — right up until you ask why someone becomes a user in the first place. Sick people get prescriptions; healthy people don't.
explain.clean: Doctors don't hand sleeping pills to random people: they go to patients with chronic pain, anxiety, depression, and serious illness — conditions that raise mortality on their own. Researchers call this confounding by indication, and it haunts every “people who take drug X do worse” claim. It runs the other way too: “people who take vitamins are healthier” — because health-conscious people take vitamins. The pills may still carry real risks; this study design just can't separate the pill from the reason it was prescribed.
explain.rule: For any “people who take X do worse”: ask why they were taking X in the first place.

sources.label: Kripke et al. 2012 — the hypnotics-mortality association (BMJ Open)
Causation or confounding? — commentary on the same association (J Intern Med)

body: Your team fires its coach after a brutal eight-game losing streak. The replacement wins four of the next five.
The pundits are unanimous: “The new coach has completely transformed this team.”
question: Did the new coach turn the team around?

statements.t: Four wins in five games immediately after the change. The improvement is public, measured, and everyone watching can see who did it.
Coaches get fired at rock bottom, after streaks that were partly bad luck, and results usually rebound toward normal whoever is in charge.
Coaching contributes nothing measurable at this level of the sport. The rebound was pure luck, and the sacking changed the eventual outcome not at all.

explain.tempting: The before-and-after is vivid, public, and exactly what everyone predicted a “real leader” would deliver. Nobody writes columns about regression to the mean.
explain.clean: Firings are triggered by extremes, and extreme stretches are usually followed by more ordinary ones — the same statistical rebound behind miracle cures tried at peak pain. Studies across football leagues keep finding that slumping teams improve after a sacking at about the same rate as equally slumping teams that kept their coach. The new coach might genuinely be better — but the bounce off rock bottom comes free either way. The same pattern sells CEO turnarounds, trading systems, and “tough love fixed my kid” stories.
explain.rule: A change made at rock bottom, followed by a rebound, is what luck alone predicts. Compare to slumps where nothing changed.

sources.label: The 'sacking illusion' — counterfactual analysis of mid-season coach changes (J Sports Sci)
Usefulness of dismissing and changing the coach in professional soccer (PLOS ONE)

body: A compilation video: electric-car fires — parking garages, charging stations, a ferry. Caption: “They want you driving a battery bomb. Gas cars never did this.”
Every clip is genuine.
question: Are EVs more fire-prone than gas cars?

statements.t: The clips speak for themselves. Battery fires are a new and growing danger, and gas cars simply never did anything remotely like this to a parking garage.
Gas cars burn by the tens of thousands each year without making the news, and per 100,000 vehicles EVs so far catch fire less often.
Vehicle fires are rare enough on both sides of this comparison — fire risk simply should not enter anyone's car-buying decision at all, in either direction here.

explain.tempting: New dangers get filmed; familiar dangers get ignored. A gas-car fire is a Tuesday; an EV fire is content. A compilation is a highlight reel of the unusual.
explain.clean: Novel risks are newsworthy precisely because they're rare — familiar risks do their damage quietly, off-camera. Insurance and fire-service data consistently show gas vehicles catching fire at higher rates per vehicle than EVs. That doesn't make batteries harmless: their fires are harder to extinguish and pose real questions for ferries and underground garages. Both things are true at once — which is exactly what per-vehicle numbers let you see and a compilation video never will.
explain.rule: A compilation counts clips, not risk. Divide by how many are on the road.

sources.label: Data comparison of EV vs. combustion vehicle fire rates (incl. Swedish MSB agency data)

body: “My cousin's eczema cleared up completely three weeks after her family switched to all-organic food. Her neighbor's migraines too.
Two people I personally know — that's not nothing. The pesticides were clearly the problem.”
question: How much do these two recoveries show?

statements.t: Two firsthand recoveries after the same change, from people you actually know, is meaningful evidence that the pesticides were the problem.
Eczema and migraines fade on their own, and millions switch diets yearly, so coincidental recovery stories must exist in enormous numbers.
Diet has no bearing on eczema or migraines. Both recoveries were coincidence, and the switch to organic food accomplished nothing for either family.

explain.tempting: Personally knowing the people feels like verification — these aren't internet strangers. But knowing them verifies the events, not the explanation.
explain.clean: Both conditions famously wax and wane on their own schedule. Take millions of dietary switches a year, multiply by conditions that improve spontaneously, and thousands of sincere “it cured us” stories are guaranteed — while the switches followed by nothing are never mentioned at barbecues. That's the anecdote filter: stories survive by being striking, not by being representative. Diet genuinely matters for some conditions — and the way to find out is comparing many similar people, not collecting the stories that happened to reach you.
explain.rule: Stories get retold because they're striking, not because they're typical. Ask how many non-stories exist.

sources.label: Smith-Spangler et al. 2012 — systematic review: organic vs. conventional foods (Ann Intern Med)

body: A phone ad shows a towering bar for its battery life next to a stubby bar for the rival's. The numbers under the bars: 31 hours vs. 29 hours.
The y-axis starts at 28.
question: What's the honest reading of this chart?

statements.t: The chart shows a dramatic advantage — the bar is three times taller — and the real numbers are printed right there confirming it.
It is a 7% difference drawn to look like 300%, because an axis starting at 28 turns two hours into a skyscraper.
A 7% gap can always be drawn to look like 300%, so the only safe practice is to ignore the picture entirely.

explain.tempting: Your eyes process the bars long before you read the axis, and the impression sticks even after you spot the trick. Designers count on the picture outliving the fine print.
explain.clean: Bar heights encode magnitude — that's the entire contract of a bar chart — so a truncated axis breaks the contract while keeping the printed numbers technically true. Two hours on a 29-hour base is about 7%: real, possibly even decisive for you, but nothing like the gap your visual memory will store. The same trick inflates stock moves, poll gaps, and crime upticks daily. The fix takes one second: find where the axis starts before you let the shape impress you.
explain.rule: Read the axis before the bars. If it doesn't start at zero, redraw the picture in your head.

sources.label: Pandey et al. 2015 — how deceptive are deceptive visualizations? (ACM CHI)

body: By lunchtime, three different friends have sent you the same alarming story about a common food additive.
“Three people independently — that's not a coincidence. Where there's smoke, there's fire.”
question: Do three separate friends sending it make it more credible?

statements.t: Three people who do not know each other flagging the same story is independent confirmation. Something real is probably behind it.
Your friends are almost certainly three endpoints of one viral wave, traceable back to a single post, so three arrivals measure spread rather than accuracy.
Stories that spread this fast are selected for alarm rather than accuracy. Rapid sharing is itself a reason to doubt the claim.

explain.tempting: In everyday life, several people telling you the same thing IS good evidence — that instinct served humans well for millennia. Feeds break it: they deliver one voice through many mouths.
explain.clean: Corroboration means independent lines of evidence. Three friends sharing one viral story is one line of evidence with three delivery routes — often a single misread study, amplified by an algorithm that rewards alarm. The forwarding count tells you the story is shareable (scary, simple, actionable), which is unrelated to whether it's accurate. One search for the original source usually settles it. The overshoot fails too: plenty of true things go viral — speed says nothing in either direction.
explain.rule: Repetition is not corroboration. Count the sources, not the messengers.

sources.label: Kuran & Sunstein — availability cascades and risk regulation (Stanford Law Review)

body: An uncle raises his glass: “Don't look at me like that — studies showed for decades that people who drink a glass of wine a day live longer than people who don't drink at all.
Moderation is literally healthier than abstinence. Science said so.”
question: Does moderate drinking extend life?

statements.t: Decades of studies found moderate drinkers outliving abstainers, again and again. That is a large, consistent evidence base pointing to a genuinely protective effect.
Those abstainer groups quietly included people who had already quit because they were ill. Correct that and the longevity benefit shrinks toward zero.
Nutrition and drinking studies rely on what people report about themselves, so their conclusions should not steer what anyone chooses to drink.

explain.tempting: The finding was real, repeated, and deeply welcome — few results in nutrition were ever this popular. Consistent and flattering is a dangerous combination: nobody audits good news.
explain.clean: The famous J-curve compared drinkers against “non-drinkers” — a group contaminated by former drinkers who stopped for health reasons and people too ill to start. Those sick quitters drag down the abstainer average, making moderate drinkers beside them look protected. A 2023 meta-analysis covering 107 studies and 4.8 million people found that once abstainer bias is corrected, low and moderate intake shows no mortality benefit at all. Note what the error was: not fake data — a comparison group assembled by health itself.
explain.rule: When a group looks unhealthy, ask how people ended up in that group.

sources.label: Zhao, Stockwell et al. 2023 — alcohol and all-cause mortality, correcting abstainer bias (JAMA Network Open)

body: Your housemate emerges, pale: “Never ordering from that new kebab place again. Ate there at eight, was up sick by two in the morning.
Six hours. Case closed.”
question: Did the kebab place do it?

statements.t: Six hours from meal to symptoms is a straight line. The last thing he ate is the obvious and most likely culprit.
Most food bugs need 12 hours to several days — norovirus 12 to 48 hours, salmonella up to six. The culprit is usually earlier.
Without laboratory testing the source stays unknowable. Working out which meal did it is not a question worth spending time on.

explain.tempting: Memory searches backwards from the symptom and stops at the most recent, most vivid candidate — the new kebab place, not Tuesday's leftovers from your own fridge.
explain.clean: Pathogens need time to multiply before symptoms start: 12–48 hours for norovirus, six hours to six days for salmonella, up to ten days for some E. coli — only a few toxins (reheated-rice Bacillus cereus, staph toxin) strike within hours. So the odds usually point one or two meals further back, often to home cooking, while the newest, most “foreign” meal takes the blame. Outbreak investigators reconstruct a full week of eating for exactly this reason. The overshoot is wrong too: with incubation windows and case clusters, sources get identified all the time.
explain.rule: Symptoms point further back than you think. Check the incubation time before blaming the last meal.

sources.label: CDC — food poisoning symptoms and incubation periods by germ

body: Under an article about drug policy: “Simple facts: over 90% of heroin users started with cannabis.
That's what a gateway looks like. Legalize it and you're feeding the pipeline.”
question: Does the 90% figure show cannabis leads to heroin?

statements.t: If nearly every heroin user walked through the same first door, that door is part of the path. The gateway effect is right there in the number.
That reverses the question, because nearly all of them started with alcohol too, and the share of cannabis users who ever progress stays tiny.
Since the 90% figure is misleading, the whole gateway concern is manufactured, and cannabis carries no health risks worth weighing in this debate at all.

explain.tempting: The sentence smuggles in a reversal: it reports on heroin users' pasts but feels like a claim about cannabis users' futures. Those two probabilities can differ a hundredfold.
explain.clean: “90% of heroin users used cannabis first” and “cannabis users go on to heroin” are entirely different claims — one is P(cannabis first, given heroin), the other P(heroin, given cannabis). The first can be high while the second stays tiny, because cannabis users vastly outnumber heroin users. (By identical logic, nearly 100% of heroin users drank milk first.) NIDA itself notes most cannabis users never move to harder substances. Whatever your policy view, this number can't carry it — and the overshoot errs equally: one bad statistic doesn't settle an entire question.
explain.rule: “Most X were once Y” says nothing about what happens to Ys. Flip it: what share of Ys become X?

sources.label: NIDA — is marijuana a gateway drug? (most users do not progress)

body: A post shows lab rats with grotesque tumors: “Rats fed GMO corn for two years. They buried this study. Share before it gets deleted.”
The photos are from a real, published experiment.
question: How strong is this evidence against GM corn?

statements.t: Photographs from a real, peer-reviewed experiment showing tumors in fed rats is about as direct as evidence against GM corn gets.
Ten rats per group, of a strain that grows tumors in old age regardless of diet, and the paper was later retracted by the journal.
Food-safety research is funded by the industry it examines, so studies on either side of this question are worth very little.

explain.tempting: Tumor photos do the arguing: vivid, visceral, impossible to unsee. And “buried study” framing adds forbidden-fruit appeal — evidence someone supposedly doesn't want you to have feels extra true.
explain.clean: Sprague-Dawley rats develop spontaneous tumors in a large share of animals over a two-year lifespan, and with ten rats per group, chance alone produces alarming clusters — the control groups grew tumors too, which the viral photos never show. The journal retracted the paper, and food-safety agencies reviewing hundreds of studies found no tumor signal. The deeper lesson is about samples: a handful of tumor-prone animals is the laboratory version of an anecdote. And the overshoot converts one bad paper into universal unknowability — its own trapdoor.
explain.rule: Vivid images from a tiny, biased sample are still a tiny, biased sample. Ask what the full evidence base shows.

sources.label: EFSA — review finding the Séralini study of insufficient scientific quality

body: A headline: “Candidate A surges into the lead, 48% to 46%.” The fine print: 800 respondents, margin of error ±3.5 points.
Your group chat is treating the race as decided.
question: What does 48–46 with a ±3.5 margin actually mean?

statements.t: Forty-eight beats forty-six, so A is ahead. The margin of error simply means the exact figures might wobble by a little.
A two-point gap inside a ±3.5 margin is fog, and uncertainty on the gap runs roughly double the headline figure. Too close to call.
Polls missed the famous upsets of 2016 badly, and everyone remembers it. Treating any of them as real information about a race this close is simply a mistake.

explain.tempting: Numbers create ranking instinctively: 48 is bigger than 46, so someone must be winning. “Surges into the lead” turns sampling noise into a story with momentum.
explain.clean: The ±3.5 applies to each candidate's share separately, and the gap between two candidates wobbles about twice as much — so 48–46 is compatible with anything from a small deficit to a solid lead. Poll-to-poll “surges” of a point or two are mostly the fog moving, which is why forecasters average many polls rather than headline one. The overshoot fails too: polls remain among the more accurate instruments available — historically off by points, not dozens — and “ignore all polls” just replaces noisy data with pure vibes.
explain.rule: A gap smaller than the margin of error is not a lead. And the margin on a gap is about double the fine print.

sources.label: Pew Research — understanding the margin of error in election polls

body: A friend sends a video making an explosive health claim: “Ten million views in three days.
If it were false it would've been debunked and taken down by now. Nothing fake survives that much attention.”
question: Do ten million views tell you anything about truth?

statements.t: Something this visible has been in front of millions of critical eyes. Claims that survive that much scrutiny have effectively been fact-checked by the crowd.
Reach tracks what makes people share — surprise, outrage, fear — and false stories do that better. Nothing about the count speaks to whether it is true.
Virality selects for emotional pull rather than accuracy, so a number this large is better read as evidence against the claim than for it.

explain.tempting: In markets and reputations, surviving scrutiny genuinely signals quality — and that instinct transfers badly to feeds, where the selection pressure is engagement, not accuracy.
explain.clean: Tracking millions of story cascades on social media, researchers found false news reached more people, traveled faster, and penetrated deeper than the truth — novelty and emotional charge drive sharing, and false stories can be engineered for both while true ones are stuck with reality. Platform review is slow and partial, and corrections travel through a far smaller pipe than the claim. Ten million views tell you a video is potent — nothing more. The mirror error fails identically: true things go viral constantly. Virality is simply orthogonal to truth.
explain.rule: Views measure contagiousness, not accuracy. The debunk always has fewer views than the claim.

sources.label: Vosoughi, Roy & Aral 2018 — the spread of true and false news online (Science)
